content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY                                    PETE WILSON, Governor


CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
45 FREMONT, SUITE 2000
SAN FRANCISCO, CA   94105-2219
VOICE AND TDD (415) 904-5200


                                                                      JUL 29 8:25 AM '91


                                                             July 29, 1991


John Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Subject:  Request for Advice Under the Political Reform Act

Dear Mr. Wallace:

This letter is a follow-up to our telephone conversations of July 26 and 27,
1991.  By this letter, I am requesting formal advice as to the applicability
of provisions of the Political Reform Act and/or Conflict of Interest laws to
my internship position with the California Coastal Commission.

I have been employed as an intern with the Commission since June, 1990.  As I
indicated in our telephone conversations, interns are state employees and are
required to complete a disclosure statement Form 730.  Generally, as an intern
within the Land Use Unit of the Coastal Commission, all of my activities are
highly supervised.  All of my written work is closely coordinated with, edited
and cleared by a supervisor.  Similarly, any public document or letter which
requires a signature must be signed by a supervisor.  Any discretionary action
which may come up in the course of my work must also be directed by a
supervisor.  Furthermore, any policy, legal, or other matter and/or product I
am involved with which may have any impact on an applicant, the public, the
Coastal Commission, or any other person or entity must be directed by, and
coordinated with, a supervisor.  In some cases, legal clearance with the
Commission's legal staff or coordination with higher officers, as needed,
occurs.

I work within the federal consistency program, which, under the federal
Coastal Zone Management Act, gives the Commission regulatory authority in
relation to federal and federally permitted activities that affect the coastal
zone (for more details on the work of federal consistency program, see James
McGrath's letter to your agency dated January 26, 1990 or the enclosed
"Federal Consistency in a Nutshell").  My major duties as an intern with the
federal consistency program have been completing several projects which are in
fulfillment of Coastal Program Significant Improvement task requirements of
federal grants the Commission has received from the National Oceanic and
Atmospheric Administration (NOAA).  The Significant Improvement products I
have worked on so far are (1) a newsletter to local governments describing the

-2-

proceedings of a local involvement/coordination workshop conducted by the
Federal consistency staff, and (2) a monitoring program for past federal
consistency projects to determine the success of mitigation measures
associated with Commission concurrence on a project.

In addition to the Significant Improvement work, I am occasionally assigned
projects to review as a staff analyst for the purposes of the Commission's
federal consistency review of a negative determination (administrative waiver
of review by the Commission, see J. McGrath's letter for details). Some of
this work involves my contact with the applicant and or other involved persons
or agencies, the drafting of letters, the writing of a staff report (with
recommendations), and possibly a presentation before the Commission. These
activities are all conducted by me within the constraints described above.
Please note, however, that no project I have worked on as a reviewer has
proceeded to the point of submitting a staff report to, presentation to, and
subsequent review by, the Commission. Instead, the few projects I been
assigned in this capacity have been withdrawn by the applicant prior to
Commission review. Similarly on the negative determinations, most of these
projects were either withdrawn by the applicant or were postponed
indefinitely. At present, I have one project before me which has the chance
to come before the Commission for its federal consistency review at the
September meeting. The previous description is purposely general in that
these activities are not the specific focus of my present inquiry, however,
they inform, general guidance in a generic sense as to how these activities
could effect future employment opportunities and/or negotiations would be
welcomed, particularly with regards to my potential staff report and
presentation to the Commission which may occur in September.

In addition to the tasks I have been assigned as described above, I have
infrequently been assigned to assist in the Commission's capacity as a
commenting agency on an EIR, EIS or other NEPA or CEQA document. These
proceedings do not come before the Commission for review, but are handled
entirely by staff. Furthermore, commenting on such documents is an informal,
optional (depending often on staff workload) coordinating activity, and has no
substantive bearing upon any subsequent formal proceeding any such project may
have at a future date with the Commission or its staff (i.e., permit, federal
consistency concurrence/objection, negative determination). The focus of my
current inquiry centers on my activities related to Coastal Commission comments
on the EIR/EIS, and other informational contacts and letter writing related to
future Coastal Commission federal consistency jurisdiction, for a proposed
tollway in Orange County.

During the period August through November 1990, an EIR/EIS was reviewed, and
comments were published by the Commission staff, for the San Joaquin Hills
Transportation Corridor (SJHTC). The EIR/EIS was published and circulated by
the Transportation Corridor Agency (TCA), a quasi-governmental entity and
proponent of the project, and the lead agency under CEQA (the federal Highway

-3-

Administration (FHWA) is the lead agency under NEPA). I was assigned to
assist Mark Delaplaine, Federal Consistency Supervisor. In this process (as a
general note, please be aware that all my activities in relation to the SJHTC
were conducted within the constraints previously described above). My
activities included reading and annotating the EIR/EIS. The annotated
document was then reviewed by Mark Delaplaine. Based on the annotations at
that point, guidance from Mark, and the policies of the California Coastal
Act, I proceeded to write a draft version of Coastal Commission comments on
the SJHTC EIR/EIS. Mark reviewed, edited, and revised the draft comments.
The final version of the comment letter, incorporating all changes made by
Mark, and signed by him, was sent to the TCA on November 26, 1990 (this letter
is enclosed). These comments were then reviewed and responded to by TCA, and
published (along with all other commenting agencies, persons etc.) in the
Final EIR (note: at that point, due to procedural and substantive problems
federal agencies had with the document, the document no longer was a joint
EIR/EIS. The NEPA requirements are therefore now being dealt with separately,
with a final EIS to be published at a later date.) The TCA certified the
Final EIR on March 14, 1991, approving the project for the purposes of CEQA.
Throughout the Commission's comment process, my activities also included
telephone contacts and inquiries with the TCA, their consultant LSA, Caltrans,
and the FHWA. These contacts were informational, either for the purposes of
answering questions about the document/project, or for
coordination/clarification on procedural aspects for our submittal of comments
(i.e., deadlines etc.).

After the Coastal Commission comments on the project were published, we began
to get inquiries from persons, organizations and other entities communicating
their concern for the project, and wondering how the Coastal Commission would
handle the project. These included the City of Laguna Beach, Terry Watt
(their consultant) and the Natural Resources Defense Council (NRDC), all of
whom were in opposition to the project (the relevance of these names to my
inquiry will be discussed below). I was advised by my supervisor on how to
handle these calls – primarily to refer them to the published comment letter,
and to inform them that the Commission had the jurisdiction to review the
project in some capacity (permit or federal consistency), but that no formal
Commission proceedings in this regard had been initiated. It is important to
note that all of this communication occurred after the Commission's comments
had been published, and therefore had no influence on the position of the
Commission as expressed in the staff's comment letter.

The next aspect of my involvement with the SJHTC consisted of researching
jurisdictional aspects of the project with regard to the possible future
Coastal Commission consistency review of the project. This also included
communication with appropriate agencies including Caltrans and the FHWA. At
the conclusion of my research and inquiries, Mark Delaplaine and I drafted,
Mark signed, a letter which explained and asserted the possible future
jurisdiction of the Coastal Commission with regards to the SJHTC (this letter
is enclosed). In addition, we have sent other similar informational and/or

-4-

inquiry letters to the FHWA, the Army Corps of Engineers, and the federal
Office of Coastal Resources Management (OCRM), all of which I participated in
writing.

Additionally, I have maintained regular contact with Caltrans and the FHWA in
order to monitor the status of any future application (or application
"trigger") to the Coastal Commission for federal consistency review for the
SJHTC. To date, no formal application process has been initiated with the
Coastal Commission by TCA. I have also served as a secondary point of contact
(other than Mark Delaplaine) for all inquiries about the potential future
Coastal Commission review of the SJHTC. Finally, the Coastal Commission
recently received a draft wetlands mitigation plan (supplemental to the EIR)
submitted for informal review, in which I have the potential to participate.

At the present time, due to budget cuts, all interns at the Coastal Commission
have been informed that our employment may not be renewed after September 30,
1991. Subsequently, I have been considering alternative employment either at
another agency, a consulting firm or environmental law firm. In recent weeks
I have learned of two possible employment opportunities, both of which are
involved with the SJHTC, and has caused me to seek advice on possible
conflicts of interest. The first of these, Terry Watt, is a private planning
consultant. She represents various organizations, governmental agencies and
public interest groups. Her work often involves reviewing and commenting on
environmental documents, plans and proposals, often times in opposition to a
project. She wrote comments on the SJHTC EIR/EIS on behalf of her client, the
City of Laguna Beach. She is representing several groups in opposition to the
SJHTC, including a law firm currently involved in a law suite challenging the
adequacy of the EIR. Her past and future projects have and will continue to
involve direct contact with the Coastal Commission. However, related to the
SJHTC, and more specifically, any possible federal consistency review by the
Commission for the project, neither she, nor her clients, have to date had any
formal proceedings before the Commission or its staff. She (and possibly her
clients) has had and may continue to have informal public inquiries/comments
about the Commission's possible future role in the project (as mentioned
above). Similarly, if the project advances to formal federal consistency
review, she may represent these parties in opposition to the SJHTC before the
Commission (either through written or personal testimony/comments), and in any
future litigation involving the Commission's decision.

The other potential employer is the law firm representing the plaintiffs
challenging the adequacy of the EIR for the SJHTC (Terry Watt also represents
this firm, including planning matters of the SJHTC suit). Again, with regards
to the SJHTC, this firm has had no direct formal involvement with the Coastal
Commission or its staff, except through any informal comments/inquiries by
Terry Watt, their consultant, and the NRDC, a co-complainant in the lawsuit.
However, should formal federal consistency review by the Commission occur,
this firm could be representing clients in opposition to the project

-5-

both during the Commission's federal consistency review, and in any subsequent
litigation involving the Commission's review of the project (permit or federal
consistency).

Please advise me specifically as to the applicability of the Political Reform
Act and/or Conflict of Interest statutes to me, an intern, with regards to
possible future employment with either of the above entities. Also, as
mentioned above, please advise me generally with regards to the laws
applicability to future(at this time unknown) employment opportunities.
Although I would appreciate broader advice as may be applicable, my specific
concerns include the following questions:

(1) If I continue to be involved with the SJHTC in the capacity described
above as part of my work with the Coastal Commission, may I seek or negotiate
future employment with Terry Watt or the law firm, while I still am employed
by the Commission?

(2) May I become employed by Terry Watt or the law firm?

(3) If I do eventually become employed by either Terry Watt or the law firm,
will there be any limitations, prohibitions or other restrictions concerning
the type of work I may perform for them (particularly regarding both of their
involvement with the SJHTC as described above)?

(4) If I become employed by either Terry Watt or the law firm, may I still
work for the Coastal Commission? If so, would I be limited in the type of
work I could be involved with at Terry Watt, the law firm or the Coastal
Commission?

Thank you for your assistance in this matter. If you require any further
information or documentation for your review of this matter, please contact me
at (415) 904-5272. Also, if possible, please advise me in some fashion
(formal/informal or otherwise) as to question (1) above as soon as possible,
as due to the possible eminent layoffs, I must begin my employment search at
the earliest possible time.


Sincerely,

[signature]
Gerard Chalmers


Enclosures
1006p
